justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Four bounded contexts identified and named (Route Planning, Fleet & Driver Management, Delivery, Customer Notification). (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified — vehicle class check spans Fleet & Route Planning contexts, recalculation triggers span Delivery and Route Planning contexts, notification window depends on route state changes. (3) Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant: true (FD-1, FD-2, FD-3), stop service time correctly marked invariant: false (RP-5), notification window threshold not marked as invariant. (4) Seven Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation.

observations.text: Exceeds minimum with 4 bounded contexts clearly delineated with ownership responsibilities
Cross-context dependencies are not just mentioned but analyzed with specific rule references (e.g., FD-1 vehicle class check at assignment and recalculation time spans Fleet and Route contexts)
Invariant classification is correct throughout — configurable values (stop time, notification window) marked as soft/no, hard constraints (vehicle class, recalculation triggers) marked as yes
7 acceptance criteria with concrete data (specific stop counts, zone names, vehicle classes) — well above the 4 minimum
Excellent edge case coverage including debounce, shift-end handoff, mid-delivery recalculation, and zero-delivery scenarios
Ubiquitous language table is thorough and precisely defined — strong DDD practice
Escalations to product team surface genuine spec gaps (multi-driver assignment, optimization objective, deviation threshold) — shows real domain authority

The Delivery Context is identified but has no business rules table of its own — its lifecycle rules are folded into Route Planning (e.g., failed delivery triggers). A stricter DDD analysis would assign delivery state transition rules to the Delivery context explicitly

Cross-context data contracts are mentioned conceptually but not formally specified — e.g., what events does Delivery publish that Route Planning subscribes to? MJ would benefit from more concrete integration point definitions

# Domain Analysis: Smart Route Optimization

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **operational dispatch and route planning process** for a last-mile delivery logistics platform. It sits at the intersection of three core business concerns: (1) efficiently assigning work to drivers, (2) dynamically responding to real-world delivery conditions, and (3) maintaining regulatory/operational compliance around vehicle-delivery matching. This is not merely a routing algorith
